summary: I was born in Dinuba, California, but I never actually lived there. My family soon moved, and I was raised nearby in Reedley. Dinuba is where my story begins, though my memories are mostly from other places.

I was raised in Reedley, California, which is close to Dinuba where I was born. My family settled there after coming from Oklahoma, and it’s where I grew up surrounded by farming communities. Reedley shaped much of my early life before I moved on for school and military service.

My parents both came from Oklahoma before moving to California. My dad’s family were farmers there, and that agricultural background influenced our family’s work ethic. Although I was born in California, my roots trace back to Oklahoma through my parents.

My dad joined the Merchant Marines right at the end of World War II after dropping out of the tenth grade. That was his way of serving during that time, and it was a significant part of his early life before settling down. His experience influenced my decision to serve in the military later on.

World War II was the period when my dad joined the Merchant Marines, right at the end of the war. Although I was born after the war, its impact on my family was clear, especially through my dad’s service. This historical context set the stage for my own military involvement years later.

I graduated from Somar High School in Los Angeles in 1967, right in the middle of the Vietnam War. It was there that I finished my secondary education before moving on to college and eventually joining the Marines. Somar was the starting point of my adult life.

After high school, I attended junior college at College of the Canyons, which is located down in Southern California. It was part of my educational journey before I eventually went to Fresno State. College of the Canyons helped me prepare for further studies and life ahead.

I went to college at California State University, Fresno, also known as Fresno State. This was after my time at College of the Canyons and before I enlisted in the Marine Corps. Fresno State was an important part of my academic background.

Nowadays, I teach at Kalinga High School, which is also where I live. It’s fulfilling to be involved in education and give back in that way after my military service. Kalinga High School is where I continue my career and community involvement.

I was living in San Fernando, California, when I enlisted in the Marine Corps. It was my home at the time, and from there I made the decision to join the military during the Vietnam War. San Fernando was the place I called home before boot camp.

I served in the Vietnam War after enlisting in the Marine Corps in 1967. I was stationed initially near Danang and later at Red Beach. The war shaped a significant part of my life, from the challenges of combat readiness to memorable moments like singing Christmas carols at orphanages.

I enlisted in the United States Marine Corps right after high school in 1967. The Marines were the only branch I seriously considered, partly because of my dad’s background. My time in the Corps included boot camp, electronics training, and deployment to Vietnam, where I served as an electronic technician.

During boot camp, my platoon commander was Gunnery Sergeant Estrada, the only instructor I really remember. He was the one who led our platoon through training, which we turned into a competitive game to get through the tough days. His leadership left a lasting impression on me.

In Vietnam, I was first stationed at the Four Corners area of Danang, right next to the airport. It was a challenging environment, especially with the strong smells and humid conditions. Danang was also where we visited orphanages and sang Christmas carols, which was a highlight during my deployment.

Later in my Vietnam service, my battalion moved to a new facility called Red Beach, also near Danang. This was part of the battalion’s relocation during the war. Red Beach became our base as we continued our operations and daily routines.

After boot camp, I went to San Diego for almost a year to attend electronics school. That’s where I learned to repair radios, which became my main job in Vietnam. San Diego was a key training location before I was sent overseas.

Before heading to Vietnam, I spent about three or four months at Camp Pendleton with the 54th Service Regiment, Electronics Maintenance Company. It was a transitional period where I prepared for deployment and participated in training exercises. Camp Pendleton was an important staging ground for my service.

At Camp Pendleton, I was assigned to the 54th Service Regiment, Electronics Maintenance Company. We maintained and repaired electronic equipment, which was my specialty. This unit was crucial in supporting Marine operations before and during Vietnam deployment.

I served in the Electronics Maintenance Company, where my job was to repair radios and other communication equipment. This was after my training in San Diego and before going to Vietnam. The company was vital in keeping our communications operational in the field.

While in Vietnam, I made three parachute jumps with the First Force Reconnaissance Company, even though Marines officially didn’t do parachute jumps. These were practice jumps, but it was a unique experience during my deployment. It added an interesting dimension to my service.

Later in Vietnam, I transferred to the Seventh Motor Transport Battalion and served as the battalion communications chief. We were responsible for loading and preparing the battalion for withdrawal after Nixon announced troop pullouts. It was a busy and critical time as we packed up and headed home.

When Richard Nixon took office, he announced the start of troop withdrawals from Vietnam. I was part of one of the first units pulled out after that announcement. His presidency directly affected the timing and manner of my return home.

We came home from Vietnam on two Landing Ship Docks, or LSDs. These ships have a big hold that can be flooded to launch landing craft, but in our case, they carried all our trucks and equipment. The trip took about thirty days, much longer than flying home, but it was a unique way to return.

On our way home from Vietnam aboard the LSD, we stopped in the Philippines for a couple of days. It was over Washington’s Birthday, and we witnessed the Navy’s 21-gun salute while going ashore. That stop was a memorable part of the long journey back to the States.

While we were in the Philippines returning from Vietnam, it was Washington’s Birthday. The Navy fired a 21-gun salute at eight in the morning as they raised the flag, which was quite a sight. It was a unique experience to be part of that ceremony so far from home.

Although I didn’t mention Bob Hope directly in my story, many Marines during Vietnam looked forward to his USO shows. Entertainment like that was important for morale, much like our own choir singing at orphanages. Bob Hope’s contributions were part of the broader support for troops during the war.
